When can a third party enforce a contrac.title: Statutory Exceptions
When can a third party enforce a contrac.text: Statutory Exceptions: The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Ordinance (Cap. 623) provides a major exception, allowing a third party to enforce a contractual term if the contract expressly provides that they may do so, or if the term purports to confer a benefit on the third party, provided the third party is expressly identified.
citations: The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Ordinance (Cap. 623)
graph_neighbors: Tweddle v Atkinson; Contracts (Rights of Third Parties)

When can a third party enforce a contrac.title: Brinkibon Ltd v Stahag Stahl [1983]
When can a third party enforce a contrac.text: Brinkibon Ltd v Stahag Stahl [1983]: For instantaneous electronic communications (telex/fax), acceptance is effective where and when it is received. (Requires retrieval)

When can a third party enforce a contrac.title: Officious bystander test
When can a third party enforce a contrac.text: Officious bystander test: A term may be implied if it is so obvious that "it goes without saying" that both parties would have agreed to it had an officious bystander suggested it

How are exemption clauses controlled?.title: Exemption Clauses
How are exemption clauses controlled?.text: Exemption Clauses: Clauses attempting to exclude or restrict liability are construed strictly against the party relying on them (the contra proferentem rule).
graph_neighbors: Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71) s.7(1)
Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71) s.7(2)

How are exemption clauses controlled?.title: Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71) s.7(2)
How are exemption clauses controlled?.text: Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71) s.7(2): For property damage caused by negligence, an exemption clause is only effective if it satisfies the requirement of reasonableness
graph_neighbors: Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71) s.7(1)

How are exemption clauses controlled?.title: Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71) s.7(1)
How are exemption clauses controlled?.text: Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71) s.7(1): Provides that liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence cannot be excluded or restricted
graph_neighbors: Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71) s.7(2)

How are exemption clauses controlled?.title: Statutory Control of Exemption Clauses
How are exemption clauses controlled?.text: Statutory Control of Exemption Clauses: Under the Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71), a party cannot exclude liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence. Excluding other negligence liability, or liability for breach of contract when dealing on standard terms or with a consumer, is only valid if the clause satisfies the statutory test of "reasonableness".
citations: Under the Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71)
graph_neighbors: Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71) s.7(1)
Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71) s.7(2)
Under the Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71)

How are exemption clauses controlled?.title: Sale of Goods and Services
How are exemption clauses controlled?.text: Sale of Goods and Services: Contracts are governed by the Sale of Goods Ordinance (Cap. 26) and the Supply of Services (Implied Terms) Ordinance (Cap. 457), which imply strict conditions regarding title, description, merchantable quality, and reasonable care and skill. Key sub-topics include the passing of property/title, the nemo dat rule (a seller cannot pass better title than they have), and retention of title (Romalpa) clauses.
citations: Contracts are governed by the Sale of Goods Ordinance (Cap. 26) and the Supply of Services (Implied Terms) Ordinance (Cap. 457)
graph_neighbors: Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71) s.7(1)

What formalities apply to land sale cont.title: Sale of Land and Leases
What formalities apply to land sale cont.text: Sale of Land and Leases: Contracts for the sale or disposition of land must be evidenced in writing under section 3(1) of the Conveyancing and Property Ordinance (Cap. 219), subject to the equitable doctrine of part performance. The framework should map Provisional Agreements for Sale and Purchase (PASP)—which are legally binding—and the vendor's implied duty to prove and give "good title".
citations: Contracts for the sale or disposition of land must be evidenced in writing under section 3(1) of the Conveyancing and Property Ordinance (Cap. 219)
